ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hyderabadi Keema; mapped to broader lamb dish class FOODON_03302757 as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (meat-based dishes) used as the closest defensible category for a minced lamb curry preparation. No exact Hyderabadi Keema entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Hyderabadi Keema or a sufficiently close minced lamb curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Hyderabadi Keema is approximately 250 g, usually accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Keema (Minced Mutton Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for mutton keema (minced mutton curry) which is the closest approved-source match to Hyderabadi Keema. Hyderabadi preparation uses additional Andhra spices and tomatoes but the macronutrient profile is consistent with the ICMR-NIN mutton keema entry. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), Table on Cooked Dishes. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174352) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat-Based Dishes and Analogous Curry Preparations — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hyderabadi Keema in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the analogous category of meat-based curry dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Meat-dominant dishes with low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) consistently fall in the low GI range (GI < 40) per University of Sydney GI Database guidance on protein- and fat-dominant foods. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (30 × 11.25) / 100 = 3.4, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 11.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~950 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 250 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 950 mg sodium, representing 41% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hyderabadi Keema has a low glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of 3.4 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~4.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~62 mg per 100 g) from lamb
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from lamb (~10.25 g per 250 g serving) approaches 50% of the recommended daily limit for saturated fat (20 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose lean lamb mince, and monitor overall saturated fat intake across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Keema is a spiced minced lamb curry originating from Hyderabad in the Deccan region of India, reflecting the city's distinctive Mughal-influenced culinary tradition blended with Andhra heat. The dish is prepared by sautéing finely minced lamb with onions, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes in oil, then simmering with a complex spice blend including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala. The Hyderabadi variant is characterised by the use of Andhra-style spicing, which imparts a notably hotter and more pungent flavour profile compared to North Indian keema preparations. The texture is semi-dry to moist, with the minced meat absorbing the spice-infused oil and tomato base. It is typically served with naan, roti, paratha, or steamed basmati rice, and is a common main course at Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, Hyderabadi Keema is a protein-dense dish (~14 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~10.8 g per 100 g, predominantly from lamb) and low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g). Its low glycemic index (~30) makes it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with certified lamb.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Hyderabadi Keema?
Is Hyderabadi Keema good for diabetics?
What spices are in Hyderabadi Keema?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mutton keema entry, which is the closest approved-source match. No exact Hyderabadi Keema entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database guidance for meat-dominant low-carbohydrate dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match was found in any primary approved source.
notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) mutton keema entry, adapted for Hyderabadi preparation style. Cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174352) for plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Hyderabadi Keema was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may have lower sodium. The dish is served at 5 Indian restaurants on field survey data in the SGD 19.90–21.90 price range. FoodOn code FOODON_03302757 used as closest_parent class; no exact FoodOn term for Hyderabadi Keema exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest_parent category. Halal status assumed based on typical lamb sourcing at Indian restaurants in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it was not reported by the primary source.
